[[Waine Pryce]] (cousin)
[[Will Pryce]] (son) }} }} '''Leon Pryce''' (born 9 October 1981) is an English [[rugby league]] coach who is the head coach of [[York Valkyrie]] in the [[RFL Women's Super League|Women's Super League]], and former rugby league footballer who played as a {{rlp|so|wg|ce}} and {{rlp|fb}} in the 1990s, 2000s and 2010s. A Great Britain and [[England national rugby league team|England]] international back, he played for [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]], with whom he had [[Challenge Cup]], and Super League Championship success, the [[Catalans Dragons]], [[Hull F.C.|Hull FC]] and the [[Bradford Bulls]]. He is a product of the [[Bradford Bulls Academy]] system. ==Early and personal life== Pryce was born on 9 October 1981, in [[Bradford]], to Jamaican parents.{{cite web|url=http://www.sportsjamaica.com/read_article.php?id%3D3696 |title=Sports Jamaica:: World Champions back Rugby League in the WI:: Rugby |access-date=27 August 2009 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20101130051527/http://sportsjamaica.com/read_article.php?id=3696 |archivedate=30 November 2010 }} He is the older brother of [[Bradford Bulls]] player [[Karl Pryce]]. His son, [[Will Pryce|Will]], is also a rugby player. His cousin, Steve Pryce, has coached in Jamaica,{{cite news|first=Dave |last=Craven |url=http://www.thetelegraphandargus.co.uk/sport/4043471.Pryce_lands_Jamaica_coaching_role/ |title=Pryce lands Jamaica coaching role |website=Thetelegraphandargus.co.uk |date=13 January 2009 |access-date=20 April 2017}}{{cite web|url=http://www.therhinos.co.uk/news/7773.php |title=Leeds Rhinos : News : JAMAICA RUGBY LEAGUE STARS HIT TOWN WITH CONNECTING COMMUNITIES |access-date=15 December 2009 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20091230075408/http://www.therhinos.co.uk/news/7773.php |archivedate=30 December 2009 }} and he is also the cousin of [[Featherstone Rovers]] player [[Waine Pryce]]. ==Playing career== ===Bradford Bulls=== At age 16, Pryce captained the [[England national rugby league team|England]] schools side. Pryce came through the academy ranks at [[Bradford Bulls]], after the club signed him from amateur side Queensbury in Bradford. Pryce played for Bradford on the wing in the [[1999 Super League Grand Final]] which was lost to [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]. He made his full début for [[England national rugby league team|England]] against [[France national rugby league team|France]] in 1999, and has also represented his home county of [[Yorkshire rugby league team|Yorkshire]]. Pryce played for Bradford on the wing in their [[2001 Super League Grand Final]] victory against the [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]]. As [[Super League VI]] champions, Bradford played against [[2001 NRL season|2001 NRL]] Premiers, the [[Newcastle Knights]] in the [[2002 World Club Challenge]]. Pryce was selected for the substitutes bench in Bradford's victory. Later that year Pryce played for Bradford from the substitutes bench in their [[2002 Super League Grand Final]] loss against St. Helens. In 2003, Pryce received 120 hours community service for unlawful wounding, after attacking former Bulls' fitness conditioner Eddie McGuinness with a glass.{{cite web|last1=Wilson|first1=Andy|title=Pryce free to play for Bradford after avoiding prison|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2003/dec/06/rugbyleague.andywilson|website=The Guardian|access-date=13 July 2017|date=6 December 2003}} He played for Bradford from the substitutes bench in their [[2003 Super League Grand Final]] victory against the [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]]. Having won [[Super League VIII]], Bradford played against 2003 NRL Premiers, [[Penrith Panthers]] in the [[2004 World Club Challenge]]. Pryce played at stand-off half back and scored a try in Bradford's 22–4 victory. He played for Bradford on the wing and scored a try in their [[2005 Super League Grand Final]] victory against [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]], winning the [[Harry Sunderland Award]] for a [[Man of the Match]] performance. ===St Helens=== After Bradford did not renew his contract in 2005, in 2006 Pryce joined St. Helens. One of the key issues in the decision was that he would start at [[Rugby league positions#Stand-off|stand off]], a position Pryce has long coveted.{{cite web |last1=Wilson |first1=Andy |title=Pryce blames Noble for departure from Bradford |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2006/jan/24/rugbyleague.sport |website=The Guardian |access-date=25 January 2026 |date=24 January 2006}} Pryce won his first trophy with St Helens in August 2006, defeating [[Huddersfield Giants|Huddersfield]] in the [[2006 Challenge Cup#Final|Challenge Cup Final]] at [[Twickenham Stadium|Twickenham]]. St Helens reached the [[2006 Super League Grand final]] to be contested against [[Hull F.C.|Hull FC]], and Pryce played at stand-off half back, scoring a try in Saints' 26–4 victory. As 2006 Super League champions, St Helens faced 2006 NRL Premiers [[Brisbane Broncos]] in the [[2007 World Club Challenge]]. Pryce played at stand-off half back in the Saints' 18–14 victory. He went on to win the Challenge Cup again in [[2007 Challenge Cup Final|2007]] when Saints beat [[Catalans Dragons]] 30–8 at [[Wembley Stadium]]. He was a joint winner of the [[Lance Todd Trophy]] along with [[Paul Wellens]]. In July 2007, Pryce was accused of squeezing [[Sam Burgess]]' [[testicles]].''Pryce accuse of squeezing tactics'' p.12 ''[[Daily Telegraph]]'' No. 47,324, 31 July 2007 He was later found guilty of the incident and banned for three matches.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/6924994.stm|work=BBC Sport|title=Pryce hit by three-match penalty|date=1 August 2007|access-date=1 January 2012}} That year he was voted rugby league's dirtiest player by ''RL Weekly'' readers.{{citation needed|date=March 2008}} [[File:Leon Pryce.jpg|thumb|right|Pryce in 2008 with [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]]]] He was again found guilty of a similar misconduct, against Bradford forward [[Andy Lynch (rugby league)|Andy Lynch]] on 22 February 2008. He escaped a possible eight-match ban as the disciplinary panel ruled the contact "minimal".{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2008/feb/26/rugbyleague.sport1|work=The Guardian|location=London|title=Repeat offender Pryce relieved not to bag a lengthy ban|first=Andy|last=Wilson|date=26 February 2008|access-date=1 January 2012}} [[File:Leon Pryce 2009.JPG|thumb|right|Pryce playing for Saints in 2009]] His success at the club continued as he helped Saints to a 26–18 win over Hull in the [[2008 Challenge Cup Final]]. He was named in the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup squads#England|England squad]] for the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]].{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/7656553.stm|title=Purdham earns World Cup call-up|work=BBC Sport|date=7 October 2008|access-date=1 January 2012}} He was named in the [[Super League Dream Team]] for the 2008's [[Super League XIII]] season.{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=11755 |title=2008 engage Super League Dream Team |publisher=Super League |date=31 December 2011 |access-date=1 January 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20100721195910/http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=11755 |archivedate=21 July 2010 }} He played in 2008's [[Super League XIII#Grand Final|Super League XIII Grand Final]] defeat by Leeds.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7642170.stm|title=2008 Grand Final|work=BBC Sport|date=4 October 2008|access-date=1 January 2012}} Pryce went with the England squad to compete in the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]] tournament in Australia. In [[2008 Rugby League World Cup Group A|Group A]]'s first match against [[Papua New Guinea national rugby league team|Papua New Guinea]] he played at stand-off half back in England's victory. In 2008 Pryce was charged and appeared in court alongside [[Stuart Reardon]] on charges of assault and violence following an alleged incident at Reardon's estranged wife's house in [[Queensbury, West Yorkshire|Queensbury]], Bradford. They were charged with using violence to gain entry and common assault. The charge of using violence to gain entry against both defendants was dropped after the prosecution offered no evidence.{{cite web|url=http://news.sky.com/skynews/Home/UK-News/Great-Britain-Rugby-Stars-Stuart-Reardon-And-Leon-Pryce-Sentenced-For-Terrifying-Assault/Article/200904415267501 |title=Great Britain Rugby Stars Stuart Reardon and Leon Pryce Sentenced for 'Terrifying' Assault | UK News | Sky News |access-date=23 April 2009 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20090426052540/http://news.sky.com/skynews/Home/UK-News/Great-Britain-Rugby-Stars-Stuart-Reardon-And-Leon-Pryce-Sentenced-For-Terrifying-Assault/Article/200904415267501 |archivedate=26 April 2009 }} In March 2009, having been found guilty of common assault, the pair were given a 12-week suspended sentence. He played in the [[2009 Super League Grand Final]] defeat by the [[Leeds Rhinos]] at [[Old Trafford]].{{cite news|last=Fletcher|first=Paul|title=St Helens 10–18 Leeds Rhinos|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/8290503.stm|access-date=22 November 2019|newspaper=BBC Sport|date=10 October 2009}} ===Catalans Dragons=== After being released by St Helens in 2011, Pryce signed for the [[Perpignan]] based [[Catalans Dragons]] for the 2012 season. The Stand-off made his début against [[Castleford Tigers]] in a 28–20 victory and was named Man of the Match. He went on to play 80 times for the French side and scored a total of 20 tries in the three seasons he spent at the club. ===Hull FC=== Pryce signed for Hull in 2015. In 2016 he was part of the team that won the Challenge Cup at Wembley for the first time. At the end of the 2016 season, Pryce announced his departure from Hull after two seasons and 38 appearances. ===Bradford Bulls=== Pryce re-signed for his hometown club Bradford ahead of the 2017 season, but the Bulls were liquidated in January 2017, casting doubt over Pryce's playing future. A new club was formed, which Pryce signed for. The new club was kept in the Championship, but started on −12 points. After 11 games Pryce announced his immediate retirement after a poor start to the season. ==Post-playing career== After Pryce retired he set up his own player agency. His son [[Will Pryce]] played for the [[Newcastle Knights]] in the NRL until moving to Hull FC for the 2025 Super League season. ==Coaching career== After retiring during the 2017 season, Leon accepted an offer to coach [[League 1 (rugby league)|League 1]] side [[Workington Town]] for the 2018 season.{{cite web|url=http://www.thetelegraphandargus.co.uk/sport/sportbulls/15597867.Leon_Pryce_lands_coaching_job_at_Workington|title=Leon Pryce lands coaching job at Workington|work=Telegraph & Argus|date=15 October 2017|access-date=16 October 2017}} ==Honours== ===Club=== *[[Super League]] (4): [[Super League VI|2001]], [[Super League VIII|2003]], [[Super League X|2005]], [[Super League XI|2006]]
